title: Model Serving Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on real-time inference, batch scoring, and managing model drift.

options.text: A model exposed as a REST API that generates recommendations dynamically in milliseconds when a user searches for an item.
A Spark job running overnight to generate predictions for millions of users and storing the results in a database for fast querying the next day.
Streaming new live transactions through Kafka into a fraud detection application.
Training a model repeatedly on multiple GPUs.

questions.question: What is a primary danger of serving a heavy ML model (like a Deep Neural Network) inside a standard, synchronous Flask app without a dedicated serving framework?

options.text: Flask cannot import TensorFlow or PyTorch modules.
Flask cannot return JSON data types.
Heavy matrix computations block the main thread, causing subsequent incoming HTTP requests to queue up and timeout.
Flask apps are inherently insecure and easily hacked.

questions.question: Which of the following describes 'Model Drift' (or Concept/Data Drift)?

options.text: When the REST API container crashes due to Out-Of-Memory errors.
When the real-world incoming data changes over time, causing the previously highly-accurate model's performance to degrade silently.
When the model takes significantly longer to load from storage.
When Hyperparameters are tuned incorrectly.

questions.question: What is the term for a deployment strategy where you route exactly 10% of live user traffic to a newly deployed Model V2 while keeping 90% of traffic on Model V1?

options.text: A/B Testing (or Canary Release)
Batch Prediction
Serverless Scaling
Single Message Transformation

questions.question: What does a dedicated framework like BentoML generate to package your model for production deployment?

options.text: An Apache Airflow DAG.
A raw pickled Python file.
A standardized, production-ready Docker container holding the API server, dependencies, and model weights.
A dbt YAML schema file.
